1: Elihu shifts audience — from Job to the wise men in the assembly. Christ also modulates his teaching audience (crowds, disciples, Pharisees, individuals) without modifying his message. The gospel is for all who have ears to hear (Matt Job 13:9).
2: Hear my words, O wise men; listen to me, you who have knowledge. 1 Cor Job 1:26–27: “not many of you were wise according to worldly standards... God chose what is foolish in the world to shame the wise.” The audience Elihu addresses — the wise and knowledgeable — are precisely those who find the cross a stumbling block (1 Cor Job 1:23).
3: For the ear tests words as the palate tastes food. The discernment of speech: 1 Cor Job 2:15 “the spiritual person judges all things, but is himself to be judged by no one.” Christ’s words are the standard by which all other words are tested: John Job 12:48 “the word that I have spoken will judge him on the last day.”
4: Let us choose together what is right. The communal discernment process that Elihu proposes — but true discernment requires the Spirit: 1 Cor Job 2:10–11 “the Spirit searches everything, even the depths of God.” The right that is truly right is revealed in Christ who is himself righteousness (1 Cor Job 1:30).
5: For Job has said, “I am righteous, and God has denied me my right.” Elihu summarizes Job’s complaint accurately. God’s ultimate answer is not to deny Job’s claim but to vindicate it (Job 42:7) — and the cross is the place where God proves he does not deny the right of the innocent: he vindicates them through resurrection.
6: Must I lie about my case? My wound is past healing though I have done no wrong. The wound of the innocent sufferer that surpasses human healing — this is the condition Christ enters. Isa Job 53:5: “by his wounds we are healed” — the wound that is past human healing becomes the source of healing for all.
7: What man is like Job, who drinks mockery like water? The exceptional sufferer who endures mockery as if it were daily drink. Ps Job 69:21: “they gave me poison for food, and for my thirst they gave me sour wine to drink” — applied in John 19:29 to Christ who literally drank the sour wine of mockery on the cross.
8: Who keeps company with evildoers and walks with wicked men. The accusation against Job — which is also the accusation against Jesus: “this man receives sinners and eats with them” (Luke Job 15:2). The one who walks with the wicked to redeem them is not contaminated but transforms them.
9: For Job said, “A man gains nothing by delighting in God.” Elihu quotes Job at his worst moment. But Christ’s resurrection is God’s refutation of this despair: “your labor in the Lord is not in vain” (1 Cor Job 15:58); the delight in God that Job doubts in extremis is vindicated by Easter.
10: “Far be it from God to do evil, far from the Almighty to do wrong.” The moral purity of God that undergirds the cross: God was not unjust in punishing Christ; he was satisfying justice on behalf of sinners who could not satisfy it themselves. Rom Job 3:25–26: the cross demonstrates that God is “just and the justifier of the one who has faith in Jesus.”
11: He repays each person according to their work and deals with everyone according to their conduct. The justice principle: 2 Cor Job 5:10; Rev Job 20:12. But the gospel exceeds mere retribution — Rom Job 4:5–6 credits Christ’s righteousness to the one who does not work but trusts. The one who repays according to works takes the penalty himself in Christ.
12: Of a truth, God will not act wickedly; the Almighty will not pervert justice. The cross is the proof: God did not pervert justice by letting sin go unpunished. Rom Job 3:26: “at the present time his righteousness might be demonstrated, so that he might be just and the justifier.” The cross satisfies justice; it does not bypass it.
13: Who gave God his charge over the earth? Who appointed him over the whole world? The rhetorical questions assert God’s aseity and sovereign lordship. Col Job 1:16: “by him all things were created... all things were created through him and for him.” God owns creation because he made it; and Christ is both the Creator and the appointed heir (Heb Job 1:2).
14: “If he should set his heart against us and gather to himself his Spirit and breath.” The contingency of all creaturely life on continued divine sustenance. Heb Job 1:3: Christ “upholds the universe by the word of his power.” Col Job 1:17: “in him all things hold together.” The withdrawal Elihu imagines is the condition that only Christ’s sustaining prevents.
15: All flesh would perish together; human beings would return to dust. The fragility of existence without continuous divine support. Christ as the one in whom all things consist (Col Job 1:17) is the answer to this fragility — and his own resurrection from the dust is the pledge that human bodies will not be abandoned to it permanently (1 Cor Job 15:42–44).
16: If you have understanding, hear this; listen to what I am saying. The summons to understanding — which comes finally not from Elihu but from the divine speech and ultimately from Christ who gives understanding: Luke Job 24:45 “then he opened their minds to understand the Scriptures.”
17: “Shall one who hates justice govern? Will you condemn the One who is righteous and mighty?” The rhetorical challenge anticipates the accusations against Christ: Pilate’s verdict (“I find no fault in him”) and the crowd’s condemnation represent the condemning of the one who is righteous. But Isa Job 9:7: “of the increase of his government and peace there will be no end” — the righteous and mighty one does govern.
18: Is it right to say to a king, “You are worthless,” and to nobles, “You are wicked”? How much less is it right to accuse God? At the crucifixion the soldiers mocked: “Hail, King of the Jews” (John Job 19:3) with contempt. But Phil Job 2:9–11 declares that this king will be acknowledged without ambiguity by every tongue.
19: He shows no partiality to princes and does not favor the rich over the poor, for they are all his handiwork. Acts Job 10:34: “God shows no partiality.” The cross is for all: John Job 3:16 “God so loved the world” — not one class or nation. The gospel reaches both the thief on the cross and the Roman centurion who guards him.
20: In a moment they die; at midnight the people are convulsed and pass away; the mighty are removed without hand. The sudden reversals of death — Christ promises the same reversal in reverse: at his return the dead in Christ will be raised “in a moment, in the twinkling of an eye” (1 Cor Job 15:52).
21: His eyes are on the ways of everyone; he sees all their steps. Heb Job 4:13: “no creature is hidden from his sight, but all are naked and exposed to the eyes of him to whom we must give account.” This omniscience makes both judgment inescapable and rescue certain — God sees the suffering of the afflicted (v28) and the wickedness of oppressors with equal clarity.
22: There is no darkness or deep shadow where evildoers can hide themselves. The absolute transparency before God. John Job 3:20: “everyone who does wicked things hates the light and does not come to the light, lest his works should be exposed.” Christ is the light that unmasks — and also the grace that forgives when the exposed person comes (1 John Job 1:7).
23: He does not impose on anyone more than is warranted to bring them before God in judgment. God’s measured justice — the cross is the most precise expression of this: “the wages of sin is death” (Rom Job 6:23), and Christ bore exactly what was warranted, no more and no less. God’s justice is not arbitrary but precisely proportional.
24: He shatters the mighty without investigation and sets others in their place. The divine reversals of power that the incarnation executes: Luke Job 1:52 “he has brought down the mighty from their thrones and exalted those of humble estate” — the Magnificat’s announcement of the reversal that Christ’s coming enacts.
25: He knows their deeds and overturns them in the night; they are crushed. The night of crucifixion is both the darkest night and the moment of the decisive overturn: John Job 13:27 “what you are going to do, do quickly” — Judas goes out and “it was night” (Job 13:30). But the one crushed rises; the night of his death becomes the dawn of resurrection.
26: He strikes them for their wickedness in an open place where others can see. The public nature of divine judgment. Christ’s crucifixion was public — outside the city gate (Heb Job 13:12), on a hill visible to all — where the bearing of the world’s sin was enacted not in secret but in full view.
27: Because they turned away from following him and gave no thought to any of his ways. The root of judgment is departure from God. The cross is God’s call-back: “Come to me, all who labor and are heavy laden” (Matt Job 11:28); “I, when I am lifted up from the earth, will draw all people to myself” (John Job 12:32). The one from whom people turn away now draws them to himself.
28: “So that they brought the cry of the poor before him, and he heard the outcry of the afflicted.” God hears the cry of the poor. Luke Job 4:18: “he has anointed me to proclaim good news to the poor.” Matt Job 25:40: “as you did it to one of the least of these my brothers, you did it to me.” The divine hearing of the afflicted culminates in Christ who is himself afflicted on their behalf.
29: “When he is quiet, who can condemn? When he hides his face, who can see him?” The question that anticipates Paul’s answer: Rom Job 8:33–34 “who shall bring any charge against God’s elect? It is God who justifies. Who is to condemn? Christ Jesus is the one who died — more than that, who was raised, who is at the right hand of God, who indeed is interceding for us.” When God is at peace with us through Christ, no condemnation can stand.
30: Lest a godless person reign and ensnare the people. The threat of ungodly rule. Christ is the ruler who does not ensnare but liberates: John Job 8:36 “if the Son sets you free, you will be free indeed.” Rev Job 20:10: the ultimate ensnarer — the devil — is thrown into the lake of fire; Christ’s reign excludes the godless one.
31: Has anyone said to God, “I have endured my punishment; I will not sin any more”? The posture of repentance before God. Luke Job 15:18–19: “I will arise and go to my father, and I will say to him, Father, I have sinned against heaven and before you.” Christ’s parable depicts the Father running to receive this confession — grace meets the returning penitent.
32: “Teach me what I cannot see; if I have done wrong, I will not do it again.” The teachable heart that asks God to reveal its blind spots. John Job 16:8: the Spirit “will convict the world concerning sin and righteousness and judgment” — Christ sends the Spirit who teaches what we cannot see and enables the repentance Elihu envisions.
33: Should God repay on your terms because you reject his? For you must choose. The ultimacy of the choice: Josh Job 24:15 “choose this day whom you will serve”; Rev Job 22:17 “let the one who is thirsty come; let the one who desires take the water of life without price.” Christ does not force; he offers — and the choice is ours.
34: People of understanding will agree with me; every wise person who hears me will say. The consensus of the wise — but 1 Cor Job 1:20–21: “where is the wise person?... God made foolish the wisdom of the world. For since, in the wisdom of God, the world did not know God through wisdom, it pleased God through the folly of what we preach to save those who believe.”
35: “Job speaks without knowledge; his words are without insight.” Elihu’s condemnation is too harsh — God himself overturns this verdict in ch. 42. Christ’s verdicts are the ones that stand: “blessed are the pure in heart” (Matt Job 5:8); “well done, good and faithful servant” (Matt Job 25:21). The final judgment of a life belongs to God alone.
36: Would that Job were tested to the limit, because his answers are like those of wicked men. Elihu’s excessive accusation. Christ is the one who is tested to the limit (Matt Job 4:1–11; Heb Job 4:15) and comes through without sin — demonstrating that the test can be endured in the power God provides.
37: For he adds rebellion to his sin; he mocks us and heaps up words against God. The final overreach of Elihu’s accusation. But God’s verdict in 42:7 shows that Job “spoke what is right.” Christ is the perfect judge of the heart (John Job 2:25) whose verdicts are not overturned.
